core_concepts: Horizontal vs. Vertical Pulling: Back training must be split into these two movement planes to hit the large, multi-muscular area of the back effectively.
Volume Landmarks (MV, MEV, MAV, MRV): Systematic management of training volume based on personal recovery capacity.
Full Range of Motion (ROM): Essential for hypertrophy; requires strict technique and avoiding momentum.
Peak Contraction: Emphasizing the squeeze at the shortened position of back movements (e.g., pulldowns, rows) for maximum stimulus.
Stimulus-to-Fatigue Ratio (SFR): Selecting exercises that provide maximal tension to the target muscle with minimal systemic/connective tissue fatigue.
Progression Methodology: Using incremental increases in weight, reps, or sets across a mesocycle rather than chasing 'ego' PRs.

actionable_advice.frequency: Train back 2–4 times per week, depending on recovery capacity.
actionable_advice.volume: Aim for 15–30 working sets per week. Start at MEV (Minimum Effective Volume) and add sets as recovery permits.
actionable_advice.intensity: Stay within the 5–20 rep range for most movements; avoid excessive work below 5 reps as it generates high fatigue with suboptimal stimulus.
actionable_advice.progression: Increase performance via small, steady PRs (e.g., 5-10lb increases) rather than infrequent, massive jumps.
actionable_advice.technique: Control the negative and avoid using the torso for momentum. Use down-sets if technique breaks down due to fatigue.

scientific_claims.claim: Hypertrophy is driven by mechanical tension, metabolic stress, and muscle cell swelling.
Optimal hypertrophy occurs when training volume is individualized to stay within the MAV (Maximum Adaptive Volume) range.
The back's multi-muscular architecture requires varied intensity (rep ranges) to stimulate all fiber types.

coaching_insights: Big backs are a long-term project; prioritize consistency and incremental overload over complex variations.
If you are sore or tired at the start of a session, you are likely overshooting your MRV and should reduce volume.
Use isolation movements for 'pump' work after compound movements to maximize metabolic stress without excessive systemic tax.
Strict form on rows is more stimulative than heavy, jerky reps that recruit the lower back or hips.
common_mistakes: Using momentum (swinging) to move heavy weight, which transfers load to the lower back and away from the lats/traps.
Overshooting volume early in a mesocycle, leading to burnout before the 'overreach' phase.
Neglecting either vertical or horizontal pulling planes in a single microcycle.
Ignoring connective tissue pain; 'pushing through' joint pain in the shoulders or elbows while pulling.
Lack of 'mind-muscle connection'—failing to feel the target muscle contracting during the pull.

motivational_quotes: A massive back isn't built in a day; it's built over years of discipline.
Control the weight, or the weight will control your gains.
Precision beats intensity every single time.
Don't train to lift; train to stimulate.
The back grows in the shadows of strict form.
If you aren't pulling from the lats, you're just pulling from your ego.
Consistency is the ultimate heavy weight.
Size is the byproduct of mastery, not just effort.
Build a back that can carry your legacy.
Rest is part of the work—respect your recovery.
myths_addressed: Deadlifts are mandatory for a big back.
You need to change exercises every single week to 'confuse' the back.
More sets always equals more back width.
Soreness is the only indicator of a good back workout.
You can fix bad form by just doing more volume.
Back width is only determined by genetics.
Heavy weight is more important than full range of motion.
You should never train your back more than once a week.
Isolation exercises are useless for back size.
You can out-eat poor training technique.

grit_application: The app should feature a 'Back Volume Tracker' that forces users to classify movements as 'Horizontal' or 'Vertical'. 1) It ensures a 50/50 split over the microcycle. 2) It tracks total sets and prompts users to stay within their MAV (Maximum Adaptive Volume). 3) It provides a 'Technique Auditor' checklist for rows/pulldowns (e.g., 'Did you control the negative?', 'Was there torso movement?'). 4) 'Load Progression Alerts' notify users when they are jumping weight too quickly (e.g., advising a 5lb increase vs. 20lb).
